resume:
Senior Growth Marketing Manager

Core competencies: demand generation, performance marketing, paid social, paid search, SEO, SEM, ABM, lifecycle marketing, marketing automation, lead scoring, attribution modeling, MQL/SQL pipeline, CAC, LTV, ROAS, MER, conversion rate optimization, A/B testing, funnel optimization, growth loops, product-led growth, retention, churn reduction, cohort analysis, omnichannel, martech stack, HubSpot, Marketo, Salesforce, GA4, Looker, Segment, Braze, Iterable, Mixpanel, Amplitude, SQL, dbt.

Experience:
Brightwave SaaS — Senior Growth Marketing Manager (2021-present)
- Own paid acquisition budget across paid search, paid social, and display
- Partner with sales on MQL-to-SQL handoff and pipeline reporting
- Drive lifecycle and retention programs via Braze
Lumen Apps — Growth Marketing Manager (2018-2021)
- Ran performance campaigns and CRO experiments

Education: B.A. Communications.
Certifications: Google Ads, HubSpot Inbound, Meta Blueprint.
jd: Brightwave SaaS is hiring a Senior Growth Marketing Manager to own a $6M annual paid-acquisition budget across search, social, and display. You will be accountable for blended CAC (currently $410, target $320), MQL volume, and MQL-to-pipeline conversion. The role works daily with RevOps and finance; you must be able to defend channel allocation with attribution data, explain why a campaign was killed or scaled, and walk through a CRO experiment end to end. We need someone who reasons in numbers: budget shifts, incrementality, payback period. Required: 5+ years performance marketing, deep GA4/Looker fluency, and a track record of moving CAC with specific, attributable decisions. The loop includes a budget-allocation case.

history.q: Walk me through the paid-acquisition budget you own at Brightwave — total spend and channel split.
What's your current blended CAC, and how has it trended over the last year?
Tell me about a specific channel you scaled. Why that one?
What attribution model do you use, and why?
Describe a campaign you killed. What signal made you pull it?
How do you think about incrementality versus last-touch credit?
Tell me about a CRO experiment you ran end to end.
What was the lift, and how did you confirm it wasn't noise?
How do you decide budget allocation across search, social, and display?
What's your payback period target, and how do you calculate it?
When CAC spiked, what was the root cause and what did you do?
How do you partner with RevOps on the MQL-to-pipeline handoff?
What's one number you're most proud of moving, and by how much?
Let's get concrete. Pick the single decision that most moved CAC last year — tell me the before number, what you changed, and the after number.

candidate_last_answer: Honestly, moving CAC is all about staying close to the data and being relentless about optimization. It's a constant process — you test, you learn, you iterate. I'd say the biggest thing is just being disciplined and channel-agnostic, letting the performance tell you where to lean in. We made a lot of smart adjustments over the year and the efficiency definitely improved. It's really about the holistic approach more than any one silver bullet.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: We made a lot of smart adjustments over the year and the efficiency definitely improved

expected-yield: force one concrete decision with a before-CAC number, the specific change made, and the after-CAC number — replacing the 『efficiency definitely improved』 hand-wave with an attributable figure
